document.title: Sage Brain
document.subtitle: Sage's own card taxonomy. The knowledge this operating system runs on.
document.notes: The Brain is the canonical self-knowledge of Sage Media & Design. It is read-only in the app and editable only via the repository. It stays lean by design — each card holds one domain, no duplicates, no bloat. v1.1 adds Card 09 — Change Orders module. v1.2 adds the email-is-a-person rule to Card 01 Identity.

cards.title: Who Sage is
cards.content_html:
Sage Media & Design is a one-roof studio for brand, websites, and AI, run by Ben Frank out of Otsego, Minnesota, serving the greater Twin Cities. When you hire Sage, you work with the owner. No account manager. No hand-offs. Every project runs through the same hands that have been doing this for a decade.

The long way here

Ben didn't start in tech. He started in kitchens — culinary school in 2012, years as a professional chef, then marketing in 2016 when family stability mattered more than the line. First agency in 2017. Narrowed to brand and design by 2019. Opened a restaurant in 2020 with his wife mid-pandemic — The Hen & The Hog — and ran both businesses at the same time. Stepped away from the restaurant in 2022 to go all-in on the agency. Added AI in 2026 when the tools got serious enough to matter.

That history is not decoration. It is the reason Sage can sit across from a restaurant owner, a chiropractor, a contractor, or a small-business founder and actually understand the work. Ben has run a business where the fryer breaks and the staff doesn't show and the marketing still has to happen. If you run a small business, Sage has been where you are.

How Sage sounds

  • Warm, direct, grounded. No hype. No selling from fear. No exclamation marks.
  • Pricing is visible. Every service page shows cost before the call. Hiding prices is disrespectful of the reader's time.
  • Verbs over adjectives. Name what gets built. Skip the adjectives about how great it'll be.
  • Real experience, named specifically. Kitchens, restaurant shifts, a decade of logos — referenced only when it earns the line.
  • Empty states have voice. A quiet room is a quiet room, not a data-less page.
  • Email is a person, not a system. Every outbound email is from Ben, signed by Ben, in his voice. No generic team signoffs. No no-reply addresses.

The positioning

Design + AI + Automation. All under one roof. Built for small businesses that need it to work — not enterprise budgets, not Fiverr templates, not a weekend in Wix. One partner. One system. One invoice.

Where Sage works

Otsego, Minnesota. Serving the greater Twin Cities area. Comfortable with any vertical a small business operator actually runs: restaurants, construction, health and wellness, service businesses, hospitality, local retail. The Twin Cities is home. The internet is the coverage area.

The signature promise

Look as good as you actually are.

cards.title: What we sell
cards.content_html:
Three service lines and one bundle. Pricing is public. Bundling earns a package rate.

Branding

  • Logo — $695. Two concepts, all file formats, primary plus alternate lockup, black and white variations.
  • Brand Essentials — $1,195. Everything in Logo, plus three concepts, a one-page brand guide (colors, typography, logo usage), and social media templates.
  • Full Brand — $1,795. Everything in Brand Essentials, plus four concepts, competitive analysis, mission and vision development, and a full brand guidelines document covering voice, usage, and application examples.

Websites

  • Starter — $1,495 ($995 during April special). 3–5 pages, mobile responsive, SEO setup, contact form, one revision round. Client provides copy.
  • Business — $2,495 ($1,995 during April special). 5–10 pages, professional copywriting included, CRM integration, two revision rounds, priority support during build.
  • Custom — $3,995+. CMS, member portals, e-commerce, custom integrations. Scoped per project.
  • Optional maintenance: $75/mo (Starter), $95/mo (Business), $195+/mo (Custom).

AI for Business

  • Entry — The Essentials. $195/mo plus $295–$495 setup. CRM, scheduling, lead follow-up, review requests, monthly report. No knowledge base. 3-month minimum.
  • Core — The Business Brain. $295–$495/mo plus $1,295–$1,995 setup. Full proprietary AI knowledge base trained on the client's business. AI phone agent, context-aware follow-ups, intelligent review responses, AI website chatbot. 6-month minimum.
  • Custom — The Full Picture. $595+/mo plus $2,495+ setup. Internal operations knowledge base, custom branded dashboard, business intelligence, priority support. 6-month minimum.

The Launch Package

Brand + Website + AI bundled. $4,495 one-time ($3,995 during April launch pricing) plus $295/mo for ongoing AI systems. 6-month minimum on the monthly retainer.

À la carte total for the same scope runs $5,785. The bundle saves roughly $1,790 — 31% off — and comes with one discovery, one timeline, one invoice, one point of contact.

Pricing philosophy

Every price lives on the public site. Bundling two or more services earns package rates. Annual payment saves 15%. Early adopter pricing is available for qualifying businesses. Nothing is hidden behind a Book a Call button.

cards.title: How we move
cards.content_html:
Sage operates on a 13-stage pipeline and three engagement types. The sales motion is demo-first: a working artifact in the prospect's hands before the first call.

The 13 stages

  1. Research — studying the company. No outreach yet.
  2. Prospect — researched, qualified, ready for a dossier.
  3. Dossier Built — dossier generated, not yet sent.
  4. Dossier Sent — email sent with the private dossier URL.
  5. Viewed — prospect opened the dossier at least once.
  6. Feedback Received — prospect submitted the feedback form.
  7. Meeting Scheduled — sales call on the calendar.
  8. Meeting Complete — call happened.
  9. Proposal Sent — formal quote delivered.
  10. Portal Activated — Activate My Portal clicked.
  11. Client Active — paid engagement underway.
  12. Partner — long-term brand partnership.
  13. Inactive — churned, paused, or lost.

The three engagement types

  • Project Work. One-time builds. Logo, site, brand system. Scoped, quoted, shipped.
  • Fractional. Ongoing embedded creative partnership. Monthly hours, predictable retainer.
  • Brand Partnership. Long-term growth relationships where Sage participates in the upside. Reserved for scalable businesses with clear trajectory.

The sales motion

The signature differentiator is the demo-first call. Before any meeting, Sage builds a working artifact using the prospect's publicly available data — a homepage concept for a website prospect, a Business Brain demo for an AI prospect, a custom dossier for anyone the pipeline touches seriously. The prospect sees their own business rendered in the proposed solution before the first conversation.

The dossier is the signature outbound artifact. Private URL, dark-editorial, eight sections long. It is not a pitch deck. It is what Sage sees in the business, what Sage would build, and an invitation to continue. Every prospect worth a dossier gets one before the sales call.

Cadence

  • Demo call — 45 minutes for AI or Launch, 30 for Websites, 15–20 for Branding.
  • Deep onboarding — one focused session covering services, pricing, policies, processes.
  • Build phase — brand first (if in scope), then site, then AI. Always in that order.
  • Launch — full pipeline tested end-to-end before going live.
  • Grow — ongoing refinement. The longer the system runs, the more valuable it becomes.

Human governance

Nothing reaches a client without Ben's sign-off. The system proposes. Ben approves. This rule does not change.

cards.title: Activated portals
cards.content_html:
Sage keeps a small roster. Not 50 clients getting 2% of attention. A handful of accounts at a time, each with enough attention to matter. Below is the active roster and the portfolio that built it.

Active roster

  • TBG Contracting — Matt Ecklund, Taking Back Ground. Commercial construction, Twin Cities plus central Minnesota. Brand Partnership. Phase 1 (logo system, palette, typography, brand guide, voice skill, six-page website, Google My Business) delivered. Phase 2 (Cornerstone brain, Base44 app, GHL, Bland.ai, Supabase KB) in progress with The G Fraction. The foundational case study for the service-business stampable model.

Portfolio — completed work

  • The Hen & The Hog — all-day eatery, vintage and warm. Ben's own restaurant. Full brand plus web build. Firsthand proof the system works.
  • Divine Life Counseling & Marriage Coaching — professional services. Elegant, minimal, sophisticated. Full brand, website.
  • Solid Rock Coding — tech company. Modern, geometric, clean. Brand identity and web.
  • Meat Daddy's — BBQ. Bold, playful, full of personality. Full brand identity.
  • Wright County Whiskey Club — whiskey club and events organization. Vintage typography, craft feel.
  • HOA Commercial Construction — commercial construction. Clean, professional, corporate. Earlier entry into the construction vertical; TBG is the expansion of that work.

Rules of engagement

  • Small roster by design. Attention is the product.
  • Long-term relationships preferred over one-and-done.
  • Every client owns their brand, website, data, and systems outright. Sage builds what Sage can hand over.
  • Industries served: restaurant and hospitality, professional services, health and wellness, tech, food and beverage, commercial construction. Open to any vertical run by an owner-operator who knows the work.

cards.title: The network
cards.content_html:
Sage does not run alone. The closest working partner is The G Fraction, and a network of vendors fills in the specialized lanes. Every partner is a first-class record.

Primary Systems Partner

The G Fraction. Led by Geoffrey Makousky (professional handle: Oki-1). Sister company, clean lane separation, no merger.

  • Sage's lane: brand, identity, website, visual system, food and hospitality work, creative direction, voice, copy, design.
  • G Fraction's lane: systems architecture, automation, CRM, AI tooling, dashboards, video, audio production, content strategy, ad operations, growth infrastructure, VA layer.
  • The handoff: Sage embeds with the client and delivers the brand. The G Fraction takes the brand and builds the back-end intelligence on top of it. Both companies stay their own companies.
  • The partnership brief: "A Working Partnership—Sage Media + The G Fraction" (April 2026). Revenue splits flexible, renegotiable, honest.
  • Shared clients: TBG Contracting is active. Scherer Plumbing & Heating, All Terrain Landscaping, and The Lab are in shared pipeline.

Vendor slots — open

Sage maintains open slots for trusted specialized vendors, engaged per project:

  • Photographers — lifestyle, product, brand, architectural.
  • Printers — large format, packaging, specialty paper.
  • Videographers — brand films, promotional, event.
  • Developers — custom backends, specialized CMS, e-commerce.
  • Copywriters — long-form, specialized verticals (legal, medical, technical).

How partner work is handled

  • Every shared client has a partner_involvement field linking the partner records.
  • Scope, lane, and revenue split are named at project open — not left to interpretation.
  • Partners are never white-labeled. The client knows who did what. Attribution is honest.
  • No partner is brought in without Ben's sign-off, even on systems-heavy projects where The G Fraction leads.

cards.title: How the work runs
cards.content_html:
Sage runs on a small, deliberate stack. Every tool earns its place. Nothing is there because it's fashionable.

Tool stack

  • Sage Operating System. The Base44 app described in these cards. CRM, pipeline, dossiers, projects, revenue, partners — all in one surface. Primary daily working tool.
  • GitHub (thegfraction/sage). The canonical brain repo. Holds this JSON, dossier JSONs, prompt templates, voice skills, and pricing configs. Source of truth for anything the app reads.
  • Claude (Anthropic). The brain layer. Used for dossier generation, daily briefings, and voice-tuned copy. Wired into the Sage OS via three documented call types (see Base44 spec).
  • Figma + Adobe Creative Suite. Design production.
  • Email sending. Resend or Postmark for transactional email (dossier sends, activations, feedback notifications).
  • Go High Level. Used on client engagements that require it (standard on the AI tiers and Launch Package).
  • Google Workspace. Core communication, calendar, storage.
  • Canva. Used only for client hand-off templates, never production design.

Workflow patterns

  • Discovery. Every client engagement starts with a 45-minute demo call using the prospect's publicly available data. No slides.
  • Phase order. Brand before website before AI, every time, for Launch Package clients. The brand carries the website; the website carries the AI.
  • Dossier generation. Each qualified prospect gets a custom dossier before the first sales call. The dossier is drafted by Claude, edited by Ben, sent manually.
  • Daily briefing. First Sage OS load each day calls the brain for a 4–7 line briefing. Format: opener, three notes, quiet close.
  • VA layer. No VA hired yet. First VA slot is reserved for a shared hire with The G Fraction. Current sourcing in motion through Alexis Krisay at Serendipit Consulting.

The human-first rule

Automation executes. Ben decides. No email goes to a client without a manual click. No dossier sends itself. No portal activates without the prospect's own action. The system proposes; the human approves. This is not a limitation of Sage's build — it is a design principle. Voice preservation depends on it.

Case study method

Every new vertical gets a proof-of-concept client at a discounted rate in exchange for testimonial rights, portfolio usage, and documentation access. TBG Contracting is that case study for commercial construction. The next verticals planned: HVAC, plumbing, landscaping, remodeling. Each will earn its own case study client and its own dossier template.

cards.title: The receipts
cards.content_html:
A decade of design work, a restaurant built from zero, and a growing roster of small-business clients who trust Sage with the brand that walks in the door before they do.

Industries served

  • Restaurant and hospitality — firsthand, including The Hen & The Hog (owner)
  • Commercial construction — HOA, TBG Contracting
  • Health, wellness, and counseling — Divine Life
  • Food and beverage — Meat Daddy's BBQ, Wright County Whiskey Club
  • Technology — Solid Rock Coding
  • Professional services — open

Featured case studies

The Hen & The Hog

All-day eatery. Vintage, warm, rustic. Ben's own restaurant, opened in 2020 with his wife and partners mid-pandemic and run successfully until 2022. Full brand, web, menu system, social — and the firsthand proof that the Sage method works when the stakes are your own livelihood.

TBG Contracting

Commercial construction, Twin Cities. Matt Ecklund, solo operator scaling toward $15M. Full brand (Sovereign purple, Restoration sage, Foundation cream, Fraunces/Inter Tight/JetBrains Mono typography), six-page website built on the Breakthrough Advertising framework, Google My Business optimized, brand voice skill encoded. Now in Phase 2 with The G Fraction building the Cornerstone brain. The foundational case study for the service-business stampable model.

Divine Life Counseling & Marriage Coaching

Professional services. Elegant, minimal, sophisticated. Brand and website built for a practice that earns trust before the first session.

Solid Rock Coding

Technology. Modern, geometric, clean. Brand identity built for a firm whose clients would see it on pitch decks and GitHub pages.

Meat Daddy's

BBQ. Bold, playful, full of personality. Full brand identity, designed to live on a food truck, a bottle, and a billboard equally well.

Wright County Whiskey Club

Whiskey club and events. Vintage typography, craft feel. A brand for an organization where every detail is expected to earn its place.

HOA Commercial Construction

Commercial construction. Clean, professional, corporate. Earlier entry into the construction vertical that TBG now expands on.

Published presence

  • sagemediadesign.com — public site. Visible pricing, full portfolio, AI demo booking.
  • Portfolio on the Branding page — six brand identities featured.
  • Case study program — every new vertical engagement is documented and made portable as a proof point for the next.

cards.title: Where we're going
cards.content_html:
The Sage model is productized and stampable. TBG is the first case study. Everything after is a variant.

Short-term — 2026

  • Lock TBG as the foundational case study. Cornerstone live by mid-June. Matt trained and operating independently inside his own system.
  • Ship three shared-pipeline dossiers to Scherer Plumbing (Delano), All Terrain Landscaping (Rockford), and The Lab (Watertown). Each is a vertical test.
  • Establish the Sage + G Fraction operating rhythm. Weekly working sessions. Clear lane discipline. Shared client handoffs without friction.
  • First VA hire, sourced through Alexis Krisay at Serendipit. Shared resource across Sage and The G Fraction.

Medium-term — 2027 to 2028

  • Expand the stampable playbook into the three adjacent service verticals already tested: HVAC, plumbing, landscaping. Each gets a proof-of-concept client, a dossier template, a case study.
  • Grow the active Brand Partnership roster to 5+ accounts.
  • Add remodeling as the fourth service vertical.
  • Open a slot for a second creative operator at Sage if deal flow sustains it.

Long-term — 2029 and beyond

  • Sage + G Fraction running 20–40 active client systems across service verticals.
  • Ben still the creative lead on every account. No hand-off. No agency growth trap.
  • Productized service-business operating model licensable to other agencies, if the demand is there and the lane still makes sense.

Non-negotiables

  • Ben remains the owner on every project.
  • The roster stays small enough to matter.
  • No generic clients, no industries Sage doesn't understand.
  • Pricing stays visible on the public site.
  • Lane separation with The G Fraction stays clean.
  • Nothing goes to a client without Ben's sign-off.

How success gets measured

Not by headcount. Not by client count. By whether the brands Sage builds hold up five years after the handoff, and whether the clients who engage still recommend Sage the next time someone asks them who did their work.

cards.title: How revisions flow
cards.content_html:
Every revision flows through one channel. Clean input from the client. Clean output from Sage. Nothing verbal. Nothing lost.

The principle

Change orders protect three things at once: scope, voice, and trust. A verbal ask in a hallway becomes a changed pricing page that breaks a calculator that breaks the sales deck. The module exists to catch that chain before it breaks.

The rules

  • Every change is human-reviewed before implementation. No auto-execute.
  • The 24-hour SLA is a review confirmation, not an automatic build.
  • If Sage needs clarification, Sage asks. Sage never guesses.
  • Every live change is paired with a downstream-effects check. Sage updates every surface the change touches, not just the one asked about.
  • Every confirmation names what changed, where, and when.
  • All active clients get unlimited change orders as part of their engagement.

The voice memo path

Clients who don't want to type can record. Sage transcribes. Client approves the transcription before the order opens. Voice is a first-class channel, not a fallback.

The activity trail

Every change order writes to the activity ledger at every transition: submitted, clarification requested, reviewed, in progress, live, confirmed, declined. The record is the receipt.

rendering_notes.surface: Dark mode default, Sage editorial style. See /json/sage-tokens.json for token source.
rendering_notes.voice: Benedict Frank. Warm, direct, grounded. No hype. Specific to the deliverable or fact named. Never aspirational without proof.
do: Let each card breathe. Generous line-height, generous paragraph gaps.
Render content_html verbatim. Do not rewrite or paraphrase.
Preserve small editorial flourishes (italic emphasis, blockquote for signature lines).
Keep the grid view compact. The detail view is where the content lives.
do_not: Do not inject dashboard-style gauges, progress bars, or KPI tiles into the Brain view.
Do not expose GitHub or JSON internals to non-operator users.
Do not add exclamation marks, emoji, or hype words in any generated adjacent copy.
Do not allow user edits in the app. Edits happen in the repo.
